preFix.title: Rabbit sore hocks: recognition and management
preFix.subtitle: Pododermatitis in meat rabbits, early signs, housing causes, and treatment.
preFix.excerpt: Sore hocks, or pododermatitis, is the most common welfare problem in rabbits housed on wire floors. The hock skin breaks down, becomes infected, and in advanced cases causes chronic lameness and weight loss. It is almost always a housing problem, and almost always preventable.
preFix.sourceNotes: Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund, housing and health guidance. British Veterinary Association, rabbit welfare factsheets. Cornell Small Farms, Rabbit Health Management bulletin.

content.text: will shift its weight off the
and sit forward on its front legs more than normal. In the early stages this is subtle; in advanced cases the rabbit is visibly lame and losing weight. The earlier you catch it, the simpler the fix.

Wire-floor housing is the most common cause. Commercial wire floors reduce contact with faecal material but concentrate point pressure on the small area of the hock that contacts the wire. Heavy breeds are more susceptible than light breeds. Damp, soiled substrate softens the skin further and accelerates breakdown. Large, active rabbits confined in small cages that do not allow natural movement patterns also show higher rates.

Turn the rabbit onto its back and examine all four hocks weekly during the daily health check. Early-stage sore hocks show as thinning of the fur over the hock. With the skin pink or slightly red beneath. At this stage the problem is entirely reversible with a change of housing surface. If the skin has broken open and the wound is moist or purulent, veterinary treatment is needed.

Remove the rabbit from wire flooring immediately. Replace with a solid board, rubber mat, or deep straw covering the wire in the resting area. Keep the resting area clean and dry; replace soiled straw daily. A rabbit with early hock damage will recover within 3-4 weeks if the pressure and moisture source is removed.

A rabbit with open, infected hock wounds needs a vet. Staphylococcal infections in the hock can spread to bone in rabbits. Which is difficult to treat and much reduces welfare. Do not attempt to manage open infected wounds with topical treatments alone without veterinary assessment.

Colony systems on grass or solid floors with clean straw have lower sore hock rates than wire-floor cages. If using wire floors. Fit a solid resting board over part of the floor so the rabbit can choose to sit on a non-abrasive surface. Clean the solid board daily to prevent faecal moisture buildup.
Step-by-step instructions for Rabbit sore hocks: recognition and management go here.
